title-group.article-title: Energetics of flow restoration after premature ventricular contractions depends on microvascular vasodilation capacity: Insights from wave intensity analysis

contrib.aff: Department of Cardiology, Amsterdam UMC, Heart Centre, Amsterdam Cardiovascular Sciences, Meibergdreef 9, 1105 AZ, Amsterdam, The Netherlands

fn.p: Conflict of interest: T.P.v.d.H. has received speaker fees and institutional research grants (not related to present work) from Abbott and Philips. J.J.P. has received support as consultant for Philips/Volcano, and has received institutional research grants from Philips (not related to present work). The other authors report no relationship with industry related to this work. The paper is not under consideration elsewhere and none of the paper's contents have been previously published. All authors have read and approved the manuscript

All other permissions can be obtained through our RightsLink service via the Permissions link on the article page on our site—for further information please contact journals.permissions@oup.com.

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Premature ventricular contractions (PVCs) are common with a prevalence of 40–75% of patients, with ≥1 extrasystole on Holter monitoring.1–3 The distinction of benign and clinically-significant cases with increased risk of PVC-induced Cardiomyopathy (PVCi-CMP)4 relies on surrogates such as daily PVCs burden, or specific predictors (e.g. R-on T phenomenon), lacking objective haemodynamic-assessment. Most PVCi-CMP develops when daily burden exceeds 10%, (particularly between 16% and 24%), yet ablation may improve systolic dysfunction with a daily burden as low as 6%.1 Thus, individualized assessment of haemodynamic impact of PVCs is needed to guide treatment strategies.

Coronary wave intensity analysis (WIA) is a contemporary method evaluating phasic cardiac-coronary interaction and energy transfer via combined pressure and flow (velocity) measurements.5 We aim to assess the impact of PVCs on WIA and cardiac-coronary interaction, including the interference of coronary vasodilation capacity with this impact, which may aid in evaluating the haemodynamic impact of PVCs.

sec.title: Study population
sec.p: This study was retrospectively conducted on the DEFINE-FLOW study.6 Detailed protocol including ethical approval (18 December 2014; METC AMC) is available elsewhere.6 Forty vessels with PVCs at resting-tracings from 38 patients were included in this analysis.

sec.title: Signal analysis
sec.p: Simultaneous electrocardiogram, pressure and Doppler velocity signals were used to calculate the net wave-intensity (WI) using our custom-software (Imperial College, UK).5 Signals were filtered with a second-order Savitzky-Golay filter. Net WI was computed with the first-derivatives of pressure and velocity signals with respect to time (WI = (dP/dt) × (dU/dt), W·m−2·s−2). WIA was automatically characterized by compression (dP/dt > 0) vs. expansion (dP/dt < 0) and accelerating (dU/dt > 0) vs. decelerating (dU/dt < 0) characteristics, yielding forward compression (FCW), backward compression (BCW), forward expansion (FEW) and backward expansion waves (BEW).7 Accelerating wave energy proportion (AEP) (previously termed perfusion efficiency8) was calculated (AEP=FCW+BEWFCW+BEW+BCW+FEW×100). Fraction of remaining energy (1−AEP) was named surplus energy flux proportion (SEP). With the automatized software, variables were independent of observer and re-calculation exhibits no numerical difference. PVCs were identified visually in resting ECG tracings. Beats with interrupted diastole were labelled as the preceding beat, beats there before were defined as the reference beat, and beats after PVCs were named the succeeding beat.

sec.title: Haemodynamic indices
sec.p: As markers of epicardial and microvascular disease, and hyperaemic vasodilation capacity, FFR, CFR, and microvascular resistance reserve (MRR) were calculated. Due to the presence of hemodynamically-relevant stenoses, microvascular resistance reserve [MRR = (CFR/FFR)×(Pa rest/Pa hyperaemic), with cut-off value of 3.0] for the definition of impaired vasodilation was used.9

sec.title: Statistical analysis
sec.p: Variables expressed as mean ± standard deviation or mean [95% Confidence Intervals] are compared using paired samples t-test, Wilcoxon-rank test, ANOVA or Friedman’s tests. post-hoc comparisons were made via post-hoc Tukey’s or The Dwass-Steel-Critchlow-Fligner tests. Pearson’s or Spearman’s correlation coefficients were used for continuous relationships as per the normality (Shapiro-Wilk’s test). P-value of < 0.05 was considered significant. JAMOVI statistical software (Jamovi Project, Australia) was used.

sec.title: Baseline characteristics
sec.p: This DEFINE-FLOW subpopulation included 40 vessels from 38 patients (70% male) with a mean age of 72 ± 8 years. Cardiovascular risk factors were frequent (smoking history: 76%, hypertension: 69%, dyslipidaemia: 90%, diabetes: 28%). Mean FFR, CFR and MRR were 0.80 ± 0.12, 2.07 ± 0.65, 2.73 ± 0.84 respectively. As markers of vasodilation capacity, abnormal MRR (< 3.0) group had 29 vessels, whereas normal MRR group had 11 vessels.

sec.title: Alterations of coronary wave intensity and PVCs
sec.p: PVCs decreased the mean velocity, which was normalized in the succeeding beats (P > 0.05), regardless of normal/abnormal MRR (Figure 1). WIA revealed altered wave-energetics with relatively increased compensation expense of acceleration in case of abnormal MRR. Vessels with normal MRR had proportionally attenuated wave amplitudes, with preserved AEP during PVCs (63% ± 15% P = 0.240) and in the succeeding beat (67% ± 17% P = 0.700) compared with baseline. Conversely, vessels with abnormal MRR had increased surplus wave energy flux with decreased AEP during PVCs (mean relative loss = −13.6%, 57% ± 12% P = 0.014), which did not recover in the succeeding beat (mean relative loss = −10.6%, 59% ± 12% P = 0.033) (reference beats: for normal MRR = 67% ± 9%, abnormal MRR = 66% ± 12%, P > 0.05) (Figure 1). Normal and abnormal MRR subgroups had no appreciable difference in WI peaks or AEP in reference beats (P > 0.05) (Figure 1).

caption.p: (A) Mean velocities were comparable between reference and succeeding beats in normal (MRR ≥ 3.0) and abnormal (MRR < 3.0) MRR subgroups as well as all-group analyses indicating normalized coronary flow. (B–D) However, abnormal MRR group had attenuated accelerating wave energy flux proportion (AEP) during PVCs, which remained residually impaired in the succeeding beat as well. In contrast, normal MRR group had proportionally attenuated WIA profile, resulting in preserved AEP during PVCs and succeeding beats. The (B) also displays example cases with normal and abnormal MRR with PVCs. Green and red areas indicate accelerating (dU/dt > 0) and decelerating (dU/dt < 0) energy flux respectively. * denotes P < 0.05, and ns denotes P > 0.05.

sec.title: Correlations with FFR, CFR, and MRR
sec.p: MRR as a microcirculation specific vasodilation marker, (r = 0.357, P = 0.036), and FFR, as an epicardial disease severity index (r = 0.374, P = 0.026), were linearly correlated to AEP of PVCs, but not that of other beats. Higher CFR was associated with better-preserved AEP of PVCs (r = 0.391, P = 0.006).

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: The haemodynamic significance of PVCs may be modified by microvascular vasodilator capacity and epicardial disease. Normal microvascular function allows rapid compensation for PVC-induced disruptions of cardiac-coronary interaction, maintaining AEP and restoring baseline hemodynamics. Thus, succeeding beats display equi-reference WIA pattern. Contrarily, vessels with abnormal microvascular vasodilation, have disproportionally decreased accelerating wave energy, and AEP decreased by ∼14% during PVCs with a persisting loss of >10% in the succeeding beat. The relationship between MRR and AEP in PVCs may indicate that flow disruption during PVCs may simulate an intrinsic haemodynamic challenge for autoregulation, explaining how the resulting impact and compensation capacity are modified by the vasodilatory capacity. Functional microcirculation seems to hyperacutely interfere with decelerative effects, minimising the cumulative impact of a PVC, which normalizes the cardiac-coronary coupling without residual perturbations.

Together with the daily PVCs burden, increased SEP for comparable flow, may reflect less effective cardiac-coronary coupling, and be associated with the cumulative impact of PVCs over a period of time. Although mentioned energy flux is a small fragment generated in the cardiac cycle, patient-level paired analyses provide an estimate of relative changes in larger-scale supporting the potential use of this index. Increased SEP may be due to any combination among severe compression of microvascular bed (=increased BCW), attenuated systolic acceleration (=lower FCW), worse diastolic microvascular decompression (=lower BEW) or exacerbated deceleration during reduced ejection phase (=higher FEW).

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: The haemodynamic significance of PVCs is modified by vasodilation capacity. WIA may identify PVC-induced perturbations in hemodynamics and the surplus work done for compensation, which may be concealed in Doppler measurements. Recovery of the baseline WIA profile and AEP may provide novel insight into mechanism of a high burden of PVCs for the development of cardiomyopathy, and it can be an objective tool to evaluate the haemodynamic effects of ECG findings, which have controversial clinical significance necessitating a more individualized approach. The clinical significance of these findings remains to be determined in larger studies.

sec.title: Limitations
sec.p: Small sample size, despite paired patient-level analysis, is a limitation. Present study did not consider the anatomical origin of the PVCs, however their effects on ventricular performance was reported to be independent of the origin.10

sec.title: Lead author biography
sec.p: Dr. Ahmet Tas, MD (Gomec State Hospital, Turkey) is a member of the coronary physiology working group led by Professor Murat Sezer at Istanbul University, where he received his medical degree. Currently, he is pursuing a PhD in coronary haemodynamics at Amsterdam UMC, co-supervised by Professor Jan J. Piek. Dr. Tas focuses on applying contemporary fluid dynamics concepts such as wave intensity and reservoir analysis in cardiovascular medicine to improve the pathophysiology and diagnosis of macro- and microvascular coronary artery diseases, for which he works under the guidance of Professor Kim H. Parker, a leading bioengineer in this field.

sec.title: Data availability
sec.p: The data of DEFINE-FLOW study is publicly available including raw haemodynamic signals from https://datadryad.org/stash/dataset/doi:10.5061/dryad.h18931zm6. The data supporting the present study can be obtained from the corresponding author (A.T., ahmettas.cor@gmail.com) upon reasonable request.

sec.title: Author contributions
sec.p: Conceptualisation and Design: A.T. and K.H.P. Data collection (primary) or analysis (secondary signal analysis): A.T., F.A.P., Y.A., I.K.T., T.P.v.d.H., M.S., and J.J.P. Statistical Analysis: A.T., Y.A., and I.K.T. Intellectual Interpretation of the findings: A.T., Y.A., F.A.P., I.K.T., T.P.v.d.H., K.H.P., M.S., and J.J.P. Drafting the manuscript: A.T., Y.A., F.A.P., I.K.T., T.P.v.d.H., K.H.P., M.S., and J.J.P. Critical Review: T.P.v.d.H., K.H.P., M.S., and J.J.P. Illustration(s): A.T. Software for haemodynamic analysis: A.T. and K.H.P. All authors approve the final manuscript and take responsibility of the presented data and the results.
